why.1: Frontmatter shows 'name: sanitizing-customer-exports' first, matching the folder, then 'description:' second.
why.2: Description starts with 'Use when a customer CSV export is about to be shared...' and ends with an immediate instruction ('Run scripts/redact_export.py on the file; never hand-edit or share the original').
why.3: Body is largely descriptive/third-person rather than imperative: 'This writes <path>.redacted.csv...and never modifies the original', 'What gets redacted' table uses noun/verb-phrase actions ('Column dropped entirely', 'Replaced with an opaque per-row id'), and the Limitations section is purely descriptive. Only the 'Before sending the file' checklist and the initial command are imperative.
why.4: No definitions of CSV or PII and no overview paragraph; the body opens directly with the command to run.
why.5: Explicit rules given: email/phone/ssn dropped, name replaced with opaque id (CUST-000001 format), notes/comments masked with [REDACTED-EMAIL]/[REDACTED-PHONE], output path '<path>.redacted.csv' next to input, original never modified — plus the script encodes DROP_COLUMNS/NAME_COLUMN/FREE_TEXT_COLUMNS exactly.
why.6: No evals.json or equivalent test-case file. Only a single 2-row sample CSV in examples/ with a prose note about expected output — no set of at least 3 cases covering normal, edge, and out-of-scope inputs.

why.1: Frontmatter is exactly `name: sanitizing-customer-exports` first (matching the folder path outputs/sanitizing-customer-exports/) and `description:` second, with no other fields preceding them.
why.2: Description starts with the trigger phrase 'Use when a customer CSV export needs to leave the company...' with concrete trigger contexts, then gives the instruction ('Redacts email/phone/ssn columns, pseudonymizes name, masks... Writes a new <original>.redacted.csv file; never modifies the original'). It is not a passive 'A skill for...' summary.
why.3: The body is predominantly descriptive/reference prose rather than imperative: the core rules are a neutral table ('Column dropped entirely', 'Replaced with an opaque id'), and surrounding text describes behavior ('Writes <path> -> <path>.redacted.csv', 'The original file is opened read-only and is never written to', 'matched substrings are replaced in place', 'This is regex pattern matching, not a full PII/NLP scan'). Only a few lines are imperative ('Run this on every export', 'spot-check the output'); the actual redaction rules are never phrased as instructions like 'Drop the email column'.
why.4: No explanation of what CSVs or PII are; the body opens with 'Redact PII from a customer CSV before it leaves the company. Fixed rules, no configuration:' followed immediately by the rules table. That single lead-in line is a directive, not an overview paragraph restating the description.
why.5: Rules are stated exactly: email/phone/ssn 'Column dropped entirely'; name 'Replaced with an opaque id (id_<8-hex>)'; notes/comments free-text email/phone substrings masked to [EMAIL_REDACTED]/[PHONE_REDACTED]; everything else passed through; output convention '<path-to-export>.redacted.csv in the same directory' with the original opened read-only and never written to. The script encodes the same constants (DROP_COLUMNS, NAME_COLUMN, FREE_TEXT_COLUMNS, output_path_for).
why.6: No evals.json or equivalent test-case file exists. The only fixture is examples/sample_export.csv with two data rows and no expected outputs, and it covers only the normal case (all target columns present) — no edge case (e.g. missing columns, unusual phone format) and no out-of-scope case as discrete test cases.

why.1: SKILL.md frontmatter shows 'name: sanitizing-customer-exports' as the first field, matching the folder name, with 'description:' immediately second.
why.2: The description starts with 'Redact PII from a customer CSV export before it leaves the company.' — a capability statement, not one of the required trigger openers ('Invoke when'/'Use when'/'Use for'/'Apply when'). A trigger ('Use whenever a support ticket...') appears only in the second sentence, so it does not START with a trigger phrase.
why.3: Body instructions are imperative: 'redact PII with the bundled script', 'Never write ad-hoc redaction code for this', 'Share only the .redacted.csv file', 'Open the .redacted.csv and spot-check', 'flag it to the requester rather than guessing'. No 'This skill does X' descriptive framing appears.
why.4: No definition or explanation of CSV files or PII appears, and there is no overview paragraph restating the description; the body opens directly with the directive to run the bundled script and then the fixed rules.
why.5: The 'Fixed rules' section states exactly: email/phone/ssn columns dropped entirely, name replaced with an opaque CUST-##### id, notes/comments scanned and only embedded emails/phones masked, original never modified, output written as '<original-stem>.redacted.csv' — explicit and exact, not loose guidance.
why.6: The only test artifact is scripts/test_redact_csv.py, a unittest of the Python script (output path, dropped columns, stable name id, free-text masking) plus a sample CSV. There is no evals/evals.json or equivalent skill test-case file, and no out-of-scope input case (e.g. a non-CSV file, a CSV with no PII columns, or a request the skill should decline).

why.6: A test file (scripts/test_redact_export.py) with 4 cases is included, but all four exercise the normal happy path on one sample CSV (drop columns, opaque id, freetext masking, original untouched).
